elements.sent-2411-001: Sloan rkenschanzstra{\ss e 17, 1180 Wien, Austria fner C\^{o te d'Azur, Observatoire de la C\^{o te d'Azur, CNRS, Laboratoire Lagrange, Bd de l'Observatoire, CS 34229, 06304 Nice Cedex 4, France.}}}
elements.sent-2411-002: The Medium-Resolution Spectrometer on the Mid-Infrared Instrument on JWST obtained spectra of three carbon stars in the Large Magellanic Cloud.
elements.sent-2411-003: Two of the spectra differ significantly from spectra obtained $\sim$16--19 years earlier with the Infrared Spectrograph on the Spitzer Space Telescope.
elements.sent-2411-004: The one semi-regular variable among the three has changed little.
elements.sent-2411-005: The long-period Mira variable in the sample shows changes consistent with its pulsation cycle.
elements.sent-2411-006: The short-period Mira shows dramatic changes in the strength of its molecular absorption bands, with some bands growing weaker and some stronger.
elements.sent-2411-007: Whether these variations result from its pulsation cycle or its evolution is not clear.
elements.sent-2411-008: Carbon stars form when intermediate-mass stars on the asymptotic giant branch (AGB) dredge up enough freshly fused carbon from their interiors to push the C/O ratio in their envelopes past unity \citep{hab96, hof18 .}
elements.sent-2411-009: They dominate the dust production in the metal-poor Magellanic Clouds \citep{mat09, mat13, boy12 , likely making them an important contributor to dust in young galaxies at high redshifts.}
elements.sent-2411-010: To understand how these stars produce dust, it is necessary to understand the chemistry and physics of the carbon-bearing molecules in their envelopes and how those molecules condense into carbon-rich dust.
elements.sent-2411-011: The Spitzer Space Telscope obtained infrared spectra of 144 carbon stars in the Large Magellanic Cloud \citep[LMC; see][and references therein]{slo16 .}
elements.sent-2411-012: The majority of those spectra were observed with the low-resolution modules on the Infrared Spectrograph \citep[IRS;][]{hou04 on the Spitzer Space Telescope \citep{wer04 , which covered the 5--14~\mum\ region for the fainter targets observed, and extended to $\sim$37~\mum\ for the brighter targets.}}
elements.sent-2411-013: The spectral resolving power ($\lambda$/$\Delta$$\lambda$) varied between 64 and 128, depending on wavelength, which was sufficient for studying solid-state emission features from dust grains and the strength and general profile of molecular absorption bands.
elements.sent-2411-014: The Medium-Resolution Spectrograph \citep[MRS;][]{wel15 on the Mid-Infrared Instrument \citep[MIRI;][]{wri23 aboard the James Webb Space Telescope \citep{gar23 provides a spectral resolving power between 2000 and 3000 and can resolve the line structure within the absorption bands.}}}
elements.sent-2411-015: We obtained time on JWST to observe nine carbon stars in the Magellanic Clouds with the MRS (Program 3010).
elements.sent-2411-016: The primary objective was to measure the resolved line structure to disentangle which molecules are responsible for the absorption at different wavelengths and to model the temperature and density of the absorbing gas.
elements.sent-2411-017: This paper concentrates on the three spectra obtained prior to the Torino XIV AGB Workshop in 2024 June.
elements.sent-2411-018: Ultimately, this project will investigate the rich molecular content in the spectral data, but this paper focuses on the first surprise, that two of the three spectra obtained so far have changed significantly since they were observed with the IRS.
elements.sent-2411-019: Figure~\ref{f.cc shows the full sample of nine carbon stars in our program and the three stars observed with the MRS between 2023 November and 2024 March.}
elements.sent-2411-020: Table~\ref{t.sample provides some background information on the three stars observed early; they are the focus of this paper.}
elements.sent-2411-021: The target 2MASS J05062960$-$6855348 will be referred to as ``J050629'' hereafter.
elements.sent-2411-022: The nine stars in the full sample were selected to sample two sequences revealed in infrared color-color space.
elements.sent-2411-023: Figure~\ref{f.cc cleanly separates the semi-regular variables (SRVs), which tend to be blue in most infrared colors, from the redder Miras.}
elements.sent-2411-024: The MRS sample includes three targets on the SRV sequence and six on the Mira sequence.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=3.4in]{tgt_cc240927.eps} \caption{The sample of carbon stars observed with the MRS in JWST program 3010 on an infrared color-color plot. The underlying photometry is from WISE and Spitzer, coded by variability classes from OGLE III. The three targets observed early (prior to 2024 June) are, from left to right, J050629, WBP~29, and MSX~LMC~736. \label{f.cc}}
elements.sent-2411-025: The IRAC data come from the SAGE survey of the LMC \citep[Surveying the Agents of a Galaxy's Evolution;][]{mei06 and its follow-up to study variability in the core of the LMC \citep[SAGE-Var;][]{rie15 .}}
elements.sent-2411-026: In Figure~\ref{f.cc , the WISE data at 3.4 and 4.6~\mum\ were shifted to the IRAC filters (3.6 and 4.5~\mum) using the color-based corrections from \cite{slo16 .}}
elements.sent-2411-027: The photometry for all available epochs are averaged after color correction.
elements.sent-2411-028: The base sample in Figure~\ref{f.cc includes all SRVs and Mira variables identified in the LMC by the OGLE-III survey \citep[Optical Gravitational Lensing Experiment;][]{sos09 .}}
elements.sent-2411-029: Figure~\ref{f.cc appeared in its original form for the SMC \citep{slo15 .}}
elements.sent-2411-030: While the details differ in the two galaxies, most likely due to their different metallicities, both show the same striking dichotomy, with the SRVs and Miras clearly divided by [3.6]$-$[4.5] color.
elements.sent-2411-031: The pulsational properties of the stars are linked to the quantity and chemistry of the dust they produce \citep{kra19 .}
elements.sent-2411-032: The weakly pulsating SRVs produce small quantities of dust, much of it SiC, while the strong pulsations of the Miras result in higher dust-production rates dominated by amorphous carbon.
elements.sent-2411-033: The full MRS sample follows the two sequences defined by the SRVs and Miras.
elements.sent-2411-034: While the SRVs tend to be blue in most infrared colors, the [5.8]$-$[8] color stands out as an exception.
elements.sent-2411-035: Because the stars are relatively dust-free, deep molecular bands can influence their color.
elements.sent-2411-036: The culprit is likely to be C$_3$ at $\sim$5~\mum\ with a possible contribution from CO, which has a bandhead at 4.6~\mum\ \citep{slo15 .}
elements.section-2411-003: Spectra from the MRS on the JWST
elements.sent-2411-037: Each MRS target was observed in all three grating settings to produce a continuous spectrum from 5 to 28~\mum, although the quality of the data deteriorates past $\sim$20~\mum, due to dropping sensitivity and, for the bluer stars in the sample, decreasing signal from the target itself.
elements.sent-2411-038: The spectra in Figure~\ref{f.spec were produced by the default JWST pipeline \citep{bus24 , which does not include a correction for the residual fringing.}}
elements.sent-2411-039: The spectrophotometric calibration of the MRS agrees with the IRS on Spitzer to $\sim$2\% or better \citep{law24 .}
elements.sent-2411-040: For each source, Figure~\ref{f.spec shows a spectrum from the MRS at its full resolution, a spectrum of the same target obtained $\sim$16--19 years earlier with the IRS on Spitzer \citep[see][for details of the IRS observations]{slo16 , and the MRS data resampled to the IRS wavelength grid.}}
elements.sent-2411-041: While some residual fringes remain in the MRS data, nearly all of the detailed structure in the full-resolution spectrum is the actual line structure within the molecular bands.
elements.sent-2411-042: The apparent position of the ``continuum'' in the downsampled version of the MRS spectrum demonstrates the challenge of determining the actual continuum in these complex spectra.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=3.4in]{cmp_3_240909.eps} \caption{MRS spectra of the three targets observed prior to 2024 June compared to the spectra from the IRS. The spectra from the MRS are also plotted after downsampling them to the lower-resolution wavelength grid for the IRS. For MSX LMC 736, the IRS spectrum has been multiplied by 1.7 to align it to the MRS data at 17~\mum\ for easier comparison. \label{f.spec}}
elements.sent-2411-043: For each target, we used the multi-epoch photometry from the SAGE surveys at 3.6 and 4.5~\mum\ and WISE at 3.4 and 4.6~\mum\ to construct light curves.
elements.sent-2411-044: The IRAC data were shifted to the WISE filters using color corrections algebraically determined from those provided by \cite{slo16 .}
elements.sent-2411-045: Table~\ref{t.sample gives the pulsation periods for the three stars investigated here.}
elements.sent-2411-046: For the two bluer sources, we used the periods provided by previous surveys.
elements.sent-2411-047: The period of 154~d for J050629 comes from OGLE III \citep{sos09 , but OGLE III also suggested a period of 741~d with a slightly higher amplitude.}
elements.sent-2411-048: The MACHO survey \citep[the survey for Massive Compact Halo Objects;][]{fra05 fitted two periods with similar amplitudes: 267~d and 238~d.}
elements.sent-2411-049: None of these alternate periods fits the WISE and IRAC photometry for J050629 particularly well.
elements.sent-2411-050: For WBP~29, we took the average from the MACHO and OGLE-III surveys (246 and 247~d, respectively).
elements.sent-2411-051: The light curves for J050629 and WBP~29 reveal a problem with our conversions of the IRAC photometry at 3.6 and 4.5~\mum\ to the WISE filters centered at 3.4 and 4.6~\mum.
elements.sent-2411-052: The supposedly corrected IRAC photometry is roughly 0.2~magnitudes brighter than the WISE photometry.
elements.sent-2411-053: A comparison of contemporaneous data at MJD $\sim$ 55400 shows the issue most clearly.
elements.sent-2411-054: The probable cause of the discrepancy is the molecular band absorption affecting wavelengths outside the region of overlap in the two filter sets.
elements.sent-2411-055: We did not use the IRAC photometry when fitting light curves for these two sources.
elements.sent-2411-056: For both J050629 and WBP~29, the adopted period was fitted to the WISE data with a sine function to determine the zero-phase epoch and amplitude for the two filters.
elements.sent-2411-057: The zero-phase epoch was then averaged between the two filters to produce the light curves in Figure~\ref{f.lc .}
elements.sent-2411-058: As explained in the next section, the estimated phases for the MRS and IRS epochs are not reliable for J050629 and are uncertain for WBP~29.
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=3.4in]{lc_3_240910.eps} \caption{Light curves for the three targets in the current sample. The vertical dashed lines for each target mark the times they were observed by the IRS on Spitzer (left) and the MRS on the JWST (right). WISE data are plotted as diamonds, and IRAC data (after conversion to the WISE filters) are plotted as plus signs. The orange and light blue curves are fitted to the red and blue data, respectively, as explained in the text. Section \ref{sec5} discusses the reliability of the estimated phases for the MRS and IRS observing epochs for the three targets. \label{f.lc}}
elements.sent-2411-059: The OGLE-III survey is biased against the carbon stars most deeply embedded within their own dust shells, because the dust extinction makes them too faint to be detected in the optical.
elements.sent-2411-060: The result can be seen in Figure~\ref{f.cc , where the OGLE-based sample fades away at the reddest colors, even though plenty of targets were still observed by the IRS on Spitzer.}
elements.sent-2411-061: Our reddest target, MSX LMC 736, is not in the OGLE-III sample, so we determined a pulsation period of 690 d from the multi-epoch WISE and (filter-corrected) IRAC data using the minimization algorithm described by \cite{slo16 .}
elements.sent-2411-062: In brief, that algorithm iterates through periods, fits an amplitude and zero-phase epoch to the photometry at each period, and returns the period with the smallest root-mean-squared residuals.
elements.sent-2411-063: The result was consistent for both WISE filters and with and without the IRAC epochs (689.7 to 690.3~d).
elements.sent-2411-064: Previous efforts using similar data and methods produced periods of 686~d \citep{slo16 and 683 d \citep{gro18 .}}
elements.sent-2411-065: The differences likely arise from fewer epochs available previously.
elements.sent-2411-066: Using completely independent data from the VISTA Magellanic Cloud survey, \cite{gro20 found a period of 672~d.}
elements.sent-2411-067: The differences between these reported periods give some idea of the overall uncertainties.
elements.sent-2411-068: Using a period of 690~d, a light curve was fitted to the available data as with the other two targets.
elements.sent-2411-069: For MSX LMC 736, the color-corrected IRAC data were also used.
elements.sent-2411-070: J050629 is the bluest target in [3.6]$-$[4.5] of the nine in the full MRS sample.
elements.sent-2411-071: Of the three SRVs, it is the reddest in the [5.8]$-$[8] color, and Figure~\ref{f.spec shows why.}
elements.sent-2411-072: It has strong C$_3$ and CO absorption at 5~\mum, and that is affecting the {{measured [5.8]$-$[8] .}}
elements.sent-2411-073: The spectrum of J050629 has changed little between the IRS and MRS epochs.
elements.sent-2411-074: Both epochs show an absorption band at 5~\mum.
elements.sent-2411-075: Both spectra also show an acetylene (C$_2$H$_2$) band at 7.5~\mum\ with its characteristic ``W'' shape.
elements.sent-2411-076: A second acetylene band appears at 13.7~\mum, or more properly, the Q branch of a wider band that stretches from $\sim$12.5 to 16~\mum.
elements.sent-2411-077: A broad, smooth absorption band from an unknown carrier is also present, centered at $\sim$10~\mum.
elements.sent-2411-078: A dust emission feature from SiC could be present at $\sim$11.5~\mum, but this apparent emission feature could just be the continuum between the two broad absorption bands to either side.
elements.sent-2411-079: The most notable differences between the two spectra can be attributed to noise in the IRS data.
elements.sent-2411-080: While the light curve for J050629 suggests that the IRS observed it at minimum and the MRS observed it 15.8 years later at maximum, the short pulsation period should limit confidence in that conclusion.
elements.sent-2411-081: The pulsation period is only 154~d, implying 37.4 pulsation cycles between the spectral epochs.
elements.sent-2411-082: Just a two-day shift in pulsation period would be sufficient to add half a pulsation cycle in 15.8 years, which would place both spectral observations at the same pulsation phase.
elements.sent-2411-083: Given that the star is a semi-regular variable, such a small shift seems entirely possible.
elements.sent-2411-084: MSX LMC 736 is the reddest target in [3.6]$-$[4.5] in the full MRS sample due to the large quantity of amorphous carbon dust in its circumstellar shell.
elements.sent-2411-085: Its light curve shows strong pulsations, with peak-to-peak amplitudes $>$ 1.0 magnitude in both filters.
elements.sent-2411-086: Its spectrum shows both acetylene bands, centered at 7.5 and 13.7~\mum, as well as an unambiguous SiC dust emission feature at $\sim$11.5~\mum.
elements.sent-2411-087: The MRS spectrum is 70\% brighter than the IRS spectrum, if one compares the two at 17~\mum, but the MRS spectrum is even brighter in the 5--12~\mum\ range, suggesting a higher fraction of warm dust.
elements.sent-2411-088: The long and steady pulsation period of MSX LMC 736 (Figure~\ref{f.lc ) results in a well-fitted light curve that shows clearly that the IRS data were obtained close to minimum and the MRS data close to maximum.}
elements.sent-2411-089: The long pulsation period and the excellent agreement between the photometry and a sinusoidal model give us confidence in this conclusion.
elements.sent-2411-090: The changes in the spectrum are consistent with the star's pulsation cycle, with the MRS data obtained during a phase of higher luminosity of the central star, leading to higher atmospheric temperatures and warmer dust.
elements.sent-2411-091: Of the three stars in the sample observed before the Torino XIV AGB meeting, WBP~29 is the enigma.
elements.sent-2411-092: While the spectra from the IRS and MRS have roughly the same brightness outside of the deeper molecular bands, the bands have changed dramatically between the two epochs.
elements.sent-2411-093: In the MRS spectrum, the C$_3$ and CO absorption at 5~\mum\ have been cut in half, while the 7.5~\mum\ acetylene band has nearly vanished.
elements.sent-2411-094: In its place is a much stronger absorption band from an unknown carrier centered at 10~\mum\ (like the band in J050629, but stronger).
elements.sent-2411-095: The low S/N at 14~\mum\ in the IRS data limits comparisons of the 13.7~\mum\ acetylene band.
elements.sent-2411-096: The MRS data show a hint of what could be SiC dust emission at 11.5~\mum, but in all likelihood, that is just continuum.
elements.sent-2411-097: WBP~29 is the bluest of the six Miras and closest to the boundary with the SRVs.
elements.sent-2411-098: Its relatively blue color for a Mira indicates that it has not yet produced a lot of dust, and that suggests that it may be in the process of shifting from an SRV, as the amplitude of the fundamental pulsation mode grows and begins to dominate the overtone modes.
elements.sent-2411-099: As \cite{kra19 have shown, this shift is associated with an increase in the dust-production rate for amorphous carbon.}
elements.sent-2411-100: In this context, the changes in the molecular chemistry could provide clues about how the circumstellar envelope is changing as the star passes through this transition to Mira.
elements.sent-2411-101: This possibility was raised at the Torino XIV AGB meeting.
elements.sent-2411-102: If the phase information in the fitted light curve is reliable, then the IRS spectrum was obtained at minimum and the MRS spectrum at maximum.
elements.sent-2411-103: That would mean that the spectral variations seen could be due primarily to the pulsation cycle.
elements.sent-2411-104: However, the light curve fitted to the data for WBP~29 in Figure~\ref{f.lc is not conclusive, and further analysis is needed for any certainty in the phases of the IRS and MRS observations.}
elements.sent-2411-105: Whatever the cause of the differences in the spectra, the molecular chemistry has clearly changed.
elements.sent-2411-106: This short contribution describes the first three spectra obtained out of nine in the total sample.
elements.sent-2411-107: The analysis here focuses on the overall shape of the molecular bands and dust features.
elements.sent-2411-108: These new data demonstrate that temporal information is an important part of any study of carbon stars, due to their pulsations and dynamic behavior.
elements.sent-2411-109: The molecular component in the new MRS data was the original objective of this project, and the data promise to reveal a great deal more about carbon stars as they evolve and ultimately eject their envelopes.
elements.sent-2411-110: The authors thank the anonymous reviewers for helpful guidance on improving this paper.
